coredata.dc:title: Modulating fluorescent sensing of the pyrene-based ion pair receptor by docking on graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jagleniec, Damian
Dobrzycki, Łukasz
Romański, Jan
coredata.dc:description: We successfully synthesized squaramide-based ion pair receptor 3, possessing a cation binding domain linked to the receptor scaffold so as act as an electron-withdrawing group and enhance anion binding. This compound was able to efficiently and cooperatively bind anions in the presence of sodium cations. Using reference compounds 1 and 2, we demonstrated that the presence of a carbonyl group on the aromatic ring significantly enhances the efficiency of anion binding. The incorporation of this group in the structure of receptor 3, combined with other strongly electron-withdrawing groups located on another aryl subunit, enabled the receptor to bind salts even in the presence of water. Furthermore, the capacity for modular synthesis of diarylsquaramides allowed to obtain sensor 4, which features a pyrene moiety directly attached to the anion-binding domain, selectively sensing dihydrogen phosphate anions. Besides the signaling feature, the presence of pyrene rings facilitated the docking of sensor 4 onto the surface of graphene quantum dots, resulting in a conjugate with improved fluorescent properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ion recognition
Squaramide
Pyrene
Graphene quantum dots
Sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Grant no. 2018/30/E/ST5/00841. From the National Science Centre, Poland. The X-ray structure was determined at the Advanced Crystal Engineering Laboratory (aceLAB) of the Faculty of Chemistry of the University of Warsaw.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Titration of receptor 3 with TBANO3 in MeCN-d3. a) Stacked 1H NMR spectra in the squaramide and aromatic C-H protons range and b) selected binding isotherms of receptor 3 for TBANO3 alone (circles) and with the presence of 1 equiv. NaClO4 (triangles).

Asymmetric part of the unit cell a), centrosymmetric dimer b), layer of molecules: view along the plane c) and perpendicular to the plane d). White and orange colors stand for different symmetry used to generate appropriate molecular fragments. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Electronic absorption spectra of 4 in CH3CN a). UV–Vis spectra of 4 taken upon addition of various anions (1 equivalent) in MeCN b).
Emission spectra of 4 taken upon addition of various anions (1 equivalent) in MeCN (λex. = 377 nm) (a); corresponding changes seen after UV illumination (λ = 254 nm) (b) and visible light (c).
Emission spectra of 4 and 4-GQDs ( λex = 377 nm).

Emission spectra of 4-GQDs conjugates taken upon addition of various anions (1 equivalent) in MeCN (λex = 377 nm) a) and histogram of changes maximum fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (500 nm for dihydrogen phosphate) of 4 and 4-GQDs upon addition various anions b).

Synthesis of receptors 1–4. Reagents and conditions: (i) methanol, 72 h, r.t., 95 %; (ii) aniline, methanol, 48 h, r.t., 71 %; (iii) triethylamine, CH2Cl2, 0 ⁰C to r.t., 24 h, 55 % (S2) and 97 % (S3); (iv) H2, Pd/C, MeOH/THF, 12 h, r.t., quantitative; (v) methanol, triethylamine, S1, r.t., 24 h, 63 % (2) and 57 % (3); (vi) dimethyl squarate, methanol, triethylamine, r.t., 24 h, 80 % (vii) 1-aminopyrene, methanol, N,N-diisopropylethylamine, r.t., 72 h, 68 %.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: UV–Vis, solvent CH3CN, 293 K, [3] = 2.25·10−5 M, [4] = 2.25·10−5 M, anions added as TBAX salt, [TBAX] ∼ 0.16 mM.

head.ce:title: Modulating fluorescent sensing of the pyrene-based ion pair receptor by docking on graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Warsaw, Pasteura 1, PL 02-093 Warsaw, Poland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Warsaw, Pasteura 1, PL 02-093 Warsaw, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We successfully synthesized squaramide-based ion pair receptor 3, possessing a cation binding domain linked to the receptor scaffold so as act as an electron-withdrawing group and enhance anion binding. This compound was able to efficiently and cooperatively bind anions in the presence of sodium cations. Using reference compounds 1 and 2, we demonstrated that the presence of a carbonyl group on the aromatic ring significantly enhances the efficiency of anion binding. The incorporation of this group in the structure of receptor 3, combined with other strongly electron-withdrawing groups located on another aryl subunit, enabled the receptor to bind salts even in the presence of water. Furthermore, the capacity for modular synthesis of diarylsquaramides allowed to obtain sensor 4, which features a pyrene moiety directly attached to the anion-binding domain, selectively sensing dihydrogen phosphate anions. Besides the signaling feature, the presence of pyrene rings facilitated the docking of sensor 4 onto the surface of graphene quantum dots, resulting in a conjugate with improved fluorescent properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Squaramides are an attractive class of compounds that in recent years have been intensively used in the construction of supramolecular systems. These cyclobutene ring derivatives have a number of features that make them outstanding for designing catalysts and self-assembly systems: they are hydrogen-bond acceptors (with two carbonyl groups), hydrogen-bond donors (NH’s protons) and exhibit aromatic characters and durable, rigid structure. Due to their ability to form strong directional hydrogen bonds these groups of compounds are often used in molecular recognition and ion transport [1–4]. The main advantage of using squaramides derivatives is their modular synthesis, enabling the sequential amidation of squaric acid esters to obtain symmetrical and non-symmetrical squaramides [5–7]. By decorating the squaramide unit with appropriate electron withdrawing or donating groups, the anion binding strength of this function can be tuned. Thanks to this ability, effective anion receptors [8–11] and transmembrane transporters [12–14] can be obtained. Moreover, by incorporating a cation binding sites nearby the squaramide function, ion pair receptors can be created [15–18]. Squaramide-based ion pair receptors have been found to cooperatively bind cation and anions, some systems are so effective that they can be applied in the extraction of salts from the aqueous to organic phase [19,20]. One interesting approach to using squaramide-based scaffold involves designing ion sensors: by linking a signaling unit with squaramide moiety, various optical [21], fluorescent [22–30] and electrochemical [31] sensors can be obtained.

As far as fluorescent sensors are concerned, the pyrene unit seems to be a particularly valuable candidate for use as a fluorescent reporter. This symmetrical polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon exhibits blue fluorescence with good quantum yield and a long-lived excited state, and due to the availability of various pyrene derivatives, it is often used as a convenient fluorophore unit [32–34]. These interesting properties make it usable in designing anion receptors [35–45], and ion pair receptors [46,47]. However, the pyrene unit belongs to the class of electron-rich arenes and its direct linking to the anion binding site hampers the interaction with anions. On the other hand, the use of pyrene derivatives and their ability to interact via π-π stacking opens up the opportunity to create other interesting assembly structures [48–51], including molecular tweezers [52,53]. This also enables the interaction of pyrene derivatives with graphene structure [54,55]. Particularly noteworthy examples of such structures are graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which due to their numerous properties find applications in many various fields such as sensing, catalysis, or production of energy materials [56–59].

Therefore, the aim of this work was to find a way to reconcile the advantages and disadvantages of pyrene: in other words, to obtain an ion pair receptor in which the problem of the electron-donating nature of this reporter is irrelevant. We envisioned that linking of the cation binding domain on the phenyl ring directly attached to the squaramide function via the amide function (electron withdrawing substituent) would assure strong binding with anions (receptor 3) even after the 3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl) group in receptor 3 structure is replaced with a pyrene unit in receptor 4. By testing the binding properties of reference receptors 1, 2 we intended to prove the validity of the design of receptor 4, i.e.:
-
the absence of a cation-binding domain discredits the receptor for effective binding of ion pairs (receptor 1).
-
the presence of an electron-withdrawing substituent enhances anion binding (receptor 2).
-
a change in the fluorescence of receptor 4 can be achieved by docking this receptor on graphene quantum dots (Fig. 1 ).

The synthesis of receptors 1–4 utilized the modular synthesis method, which relies on the successive amidation of commercially available dimethyl squarate with corresponding amines (Scheme 1 ). The monotopic anion receptor 1 was synthesized starting with amidation of dimethyl squarate with 3,5-trifluoromethylaniline to give monosquaramide S1, followed by a second amidation with aniline. The synthesis of receptors 2 and 3 involved acyl substitution of 3-nitrobenzoyl chloride with morpholine and 1-aza-18-crown-6 ether, respectively. Reduction of nitro groups in compounds S2, S3 was accomplished using hydrogen (1 atm) and Pd/C catalyst, yielding the corresponding amines A2, A3. The obtained amines A2, and A3 were reacted with monosquaramide S1 affording receptors 2 and 3. In order to synthesize ion pair sensor 4, amine A3 was reacted with dimethyl squarate yielding monosquaramide S4, followed by a second amidation with 1-aminopyrene.

With all receptors in hand anion and salt binding studies were performed by means of UV–Vis titrations in acetonitrile. Initially, dilution experiments were carried out, which excluded the self-association of receptors 1–4 in the tested concentration range (Figure S1-S4). We selected interaction with bromide salt to screen the binding properties of all the obtained receptors and demonstrate the role of particular functional groups in the receptor structures. We used tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBABr) as an anion source. In order to check the binding ability of the receptors towards sodium bromide, analogous titration experiments were performed in the presence of one equivalent of sodium perchlorate, thus generating this salt in situ. The addition of tetrabutylammonium perchlorate to a solution of receptor 3 in acetonitrile did not affect its spectra indicating that these ions do not interact with binding domains in such conditions. The obtained UV–Vis titration curves were fitted with a 1:1 complex model using the open source software BindFit. The results in the form of association constants calculated by nonlinear regression analysis of titration data are listed in Table 1 .

The stability constants listed in Table 1 show some interesting trends. First, monotopic receptor 1 recognizes bromide anions distinctly more weakly than monotopic receptor 2. This is due to the presence of an electron-withdrawing carbonyl group located on the phenyl ring in the structure of receptor 2, which greatly contributes to increased acidity squaramide protons and consequently translates into a stronger binding of bromide. This also refers to ion pair receptor 3, which interacts with bromide anions similarly to receptor 2. This is quite logical, as their difference in structure has no direct influence on the anion-binding domain. As expected, receptor 4 equipped with a pyrene unit recognizes bromide anions most weakly due to the electron-donating nature of this substituent. However, when we carried out titration experiments in the presence of sodium cations (added as sodium perchlorate) the ability of receptors 1–4 to recognize bromide anions changed. Specifically, both the monotopic anion receptors 1 and 2 were able to recognize bromide anions less effectively in the presence of sodium cations, most likely as a result of competition in anion binding between receptors and sodium cation in solution (1 and 2 lack of cation binding domain). On the other hand, receptors 3 and 4 recognize bromide anions in an enhanced manner. This is due to the ditopic nature of these receptors and their ability to recognize anions and cations simultaneously. Thanks to the electron-withdrawing nature of the bis-3,5-trifluoromethylphenyl substituent in the structure of receptor 3, it recognizes bromide anions and in situ generated sodium salts more effectively than receptor 4. Therefore, we extended titration experiments to include other salts and determined stability constants for complexes of receptor 3 (Table 2 ).

Receptor 3 binds the tested anions in the following series: Cl− > Br− > I− > NO3 −. Chlorides were found to be recognized most strongly by receptor 3. This is in agreement with previous work reporting the interaction of diarylsquaramides with halide anions and the additional CH...Cl− interaction upon complex formation [60]. In the case of titration of 3 with acetate, benzoate, and dihydrogen phosphate anions, a new bathochromic band centered at 380 nm appeared, most likely as a result of partial deprotonation of the receptor (Figure S20-S22). The deprotonation event was proved by titration experiments of 3 with tetrabutylammonium hydroxide, which showed that after the addition of TBAOH to the solution of 3 the same band appeared (Figure S5). In the presence of sodium cations, an enhancement in anion binding was noted. The enhancement factor KNa+/KTBA+, defined as a ratio of stability constant for complexes of 3 with in situ generated sodium salts and with anions, was found to be the greatest for nitrate anions. Encouraged by a strong affinity of receptor 3 towards chlorides, we decided to verify whether recognition events take place also in an aqueous environment. For this reason, we conducted UV–Vis titration experiments in acetonitrile with 5 % water, which made it possible to use sodium chloride instead of salt generated in situ. We found that receptor 3 binds chloride anions (added as TBACl) with an association constant of K TBACl = 1300 ± 20 M−1. Notably, NaCl was bound more strongly than TBACl, namely with an association constant of K NaCl = 1650 ± 20 M−1.

To better understand the binding mechanism of receptor 3, 1H NMR titration experiments in CD3CN were conducted (Fig. 2 ). Nitrate salt was selected for testing due to the estimated values of the stability constant appropriate for the 1H NMR titration technique and in order to confirm a high enhancement factor for nitrates by an independent method. The addition of TBANO3 salt to a solution of 3 in CD3CN caused a downfield shift Δδ = 1.92 and 2.45 ppm of both NH signals corresponding to the squaramide unit clearly proving the participation of this domain in anion binding. Less pronounced changes of chemical shifts for the signals corresponding to aromatic protons were also noted as a result of electron density change upon nitrate anion complexation. Upon the addition of sodium cations to the solution of 3 in CD3CN, a perturbation of signals corresponding to the crown ether was found, indicating the interaction of receptor 3 with this binding domain. In the presence of cations, the changes in chemical shifts induced by the addition of anions were more prominent suggesting stronger interactions with ions. Indeed, stability constants were calculated to be K TBANO3 = 2200 ± 42 M−1, and in the presence of 1 eq. NaClO4 K NaNO3 = 7500 ± 700 M−1. These data correspond well with data obtained from UV–Vis titration.

Single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis of ligand 3 with NaBr confirms the salt complexation in the solid state. Sodium cations are trapped in the monoaza-18-crown-6 moiety and Br− anions are hydrogen-bonded to the squaramide binding domain of 3. The crystal exhibits exact 1:1 ligand:salt stoichiometry. The crystal contains additional water and methanol species coordinating sodium cations. The ligand molecule exhibits a minor disorder of fluorine atoms in one CF3 group, whereas the positions of the other atoms are well defined. The monoaza-18-crown-6 moiety is significantly twisted in the N-containing part and has a pseudo two-fold symmetry. Only the oxygen atoms of the crown ether coordinate the sodium ion. All the O and Na+ species are located close to a least-squares plane fitted to these moieties with the maximum deviation of 0.211(1) Å. The sodium ion, at the opposite side of the crown ether moiety, is additionally coordinated by oxygen atoms of the methanol and water molecules, thus the cation complexation polyhedron corresponds to a distorted pentagonal bipyramid. In the complex the
O ligand
-
Na
central ion
+
distances fall in range of 2.350(2) Å to 2.594(2) Å. The asymmetric part of the unit cell of 3×NaBr is presented in (Fig. 3 a) with the cationic site displayed in polyhedral representation.

In the crystal lattice Br− ion coordinated by the squaramide N-H moieties of the 3 ligand is engaged in an additional hydrogen bond with an OH group of the methanol molecule present in the neighboring 3 ligand. These two ligands are related by symmetry, resulting in the formation of a centrosymmetric dimer, presented in (Fig. 3b). Different colors here stand for two symmetry operations. The coordination of the Br− ion is highly anisotropic. The angle between the hydrogen bond donors: squaramide N-H groups and OH moiety is close to 90°. Such anisotropy of Br− ion coordination seems to be typical for known similar ligand complexes in the solid state [18,28,29]. The centrosymmetric dimers are linked together in layers by additional hydrogen bonds between water molecules and carbonyl O atoms of the squaramide unit. These layers parallel to the (001) lattice plane are decorated at the boundaries by aliphatic fragments of the crown ether moieties and by CF3 groups, as is presented in (Fig. 3c and 3d).

Sensing properties of the designed receptors was achieved by linking the pyrene unit directly with squaramide function in the structure of receptor 4 [61]. Although the pyrene unit belongs to the class of electron-rich arenes the proper acidity of the squaramide-based anion binding domain was assured by the presence of the second aryl substituent linked with the cation binding domain, so as to act as an electron-withdrawing group.

Initially, the interaction of receptor 4 with the tested anions was studied by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in acetonitrile. The UV–Vis spectrum of 4 presents four absorption bands: 240 nm, 277 nm, 316 nm and 377 nm with the latter being associated with the squaramide function (Fig. 4 a). Upon the addition of selected anions (added as TBA salts) to the 2·10−5 M solution of 4 in acetonitrile, a bathochromic shift in the registered spectrum was observed (Fig. 4b). The most pronounced red shift was found for the band centered at 377 nm. This was attributed to the formation of complexes of 4 with anions involving hydrogen bonding of squaramide function with anionic species. In the case of the addition of carboxylate, apart from the aforementioned changes in the UV–Vis spectrum, a formation of a new band at 450 nm and solution color change for intensive yellow was observed (Fig. 5 c). The new band at 450 nm was also registered upon the addition of TBAOH to the solution of 4 suggesting deprotonation of receptor 4, upon interaction with basic anions (Figure S32). The band at 450 nm was found to be the highest for the addition of acetate anions which may suggest the highest fraction of deprotonated receptor 4 in the solution in this case. Formation of a new band at 450 nm, although much less pronounced, was noted upon the addition of dihydrogen phosphate anions to the solution of 4, suggesting that the contribution of deprotonation to the interaction with the receptor is negligible in this case, and the receptor mainly forms a durable complex with this anion. This was confirmed by the fact that the most pronounced red shift of the band centered at 377 nm was noted after adding the dihydrogen phosphate anion to the solution of 4 (Fig. 4b).

Upon the addition of one equivalent of anions to the solution of 4 in MeCN an increase in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 5a). This effect was distinctly smaller for the addition of bromide and iodide anions than for chlorides and correlates with the binding strength of receptor 4 with halide anions (Table 2). Thanks to this, it was possible to detect chloride anions selectively within tested halides with the 'naked eye' using a UV–Vis lamp after illumination with λ = 254 nm (Fig. 5b). On the other hand, the addition of carboxylate anions to the solution of 4 produced a new band at 550 nm, which was attributed to the deprotonation event (Fig. S35) and produced a different optical output. Due to this, it was also possible to distinguish the interaction of carboxylates with 4 from the rest of the anions tested (Fig. 5b, c). The addition of dihydrogen phosphate anions to the solution of 4 resulted in a clearly improved enhancement in fluorescence intensity, supporting the findings of the formation of strong complexes of 4 with this anion, as taken from UV–Vis experiments. This allowed for the most pronounced visible detection of dihydrogen phosphate from all anions tested. However, adding an excess of dihydrogen phosphate anions to the solution of 4 resulted in a more pronounced formation of the band at 550 nm, along with decreasing the band attributable to complex formation, suggesting that the excess of these anions favors the deprotonation of the receptor (Fig. S39). Studies conducted in the presence of cations for compound 4 resulted in a slight increase in emission bands, especially noted for the interaction of 4 with halide anions, without significantly affecting the detection of salts with the naked eye (Fig. S40).

We then shifted our attention to using a different property of pyrene and exploiting its ability to form noncovalent interactions with polyaromatic systems. We intend to verify whether the formation of the new supramolecular conjugate can affect anion sensing. For this purpose, we docked sensor 4 on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), obtaining conjugates 4-GQDs. The first evidence of the docking of 4 on GQDs came from 1H NMR spectroscopy. Upon the addition of GQDs to the solution of 4 in CD3CN the signal corresponding to the squaramide proton was shifted upfield suggesting that docking 4 on GQDs changed the pyrene moiety to be an even more electron donating system thus reducing the squaramide proton acidity (Fig. S45). The perturbation in signals corresponding to the aromatic protons was also noted most likely as the result of the formation of π-π interaction of 4 with GQDs. Moreover, for the 4-GQDs conjugate, an increase in the intensity of the emission band in relation to receptor 4 and the disappearance of the band of GQDs was observed (Fig. 6 ). The reduction in the acidity of squaramide protons in 4-GQDs was confirmed by a titration experiment carried out under UV–Vis spectroscopy control. Specifically, we noted that chloride anion was less effectively recognized by 4- G Q D s conjugate (Ka = 156400 ± 10000 M−1) than by receptor 4 itself (Ka = 288900 ± 8600 M−1).

Next, we investigated the fluorescence properties of the 4-GQDs conjugate upon interaction with tested anions. The 4-GQDs conjugate is characterized by a more intense emission band than 4 itself, and upon the addition of anions, a different optical response was observed in comparison to the interaction of 4 with anions (Fig. 7 a and 7b). Specifically, upon the addition of halide and dihydrogen phosphate anions to the solution of 4-GQDs, an enhanced increase in fluorescence intensity was observed. In the case of 4- G Q Ds, upon the addition of carboxylates, the ratio in the intensity of the 450 nm band, corresponding to the formation of complexes with anions, increases in favor of the intensity of the 550 nm band, attributed to deprotonation. This may suggest that, after docking receptor 4 on GQDs, squaramides protons are much less prone to deprotonation. This could be due to their lower acidity or steric hindrance, significantly impeding anions like acetate and benzoate from removing these protons. Confirmation of this fact is also observed by the UV–Vis spectra, where the 450 nm band assigned to a deprotonated receptor is much smaller in the case of 4-GQDs upon addition of carboxylates (Figure S33 and S34). Taken together docking the receptor to GQDs resulted in an increased emission response toward the anions tested relative to 4 alone as well as a reduction in deprotonation of anion binding site by basic anions.

In summary, a novel N, N-diarylsquaramide ion pair receptor 3 was obtained which possesses electron-withdrawing groups on both phenyl rings. One of them is a cation-binding domain linked via the amide function. It was demonstrated that attaching this binding domain through a carbonyl group significantly increased the binding strength of anions by the receptor, enabling the recognition of salts even under highly competitive aqueous conditions. The method of attaching the cation-binding domain allowed for the direct attachment of a pyrene unit to the anion-binding domain to create a fluorescent sensor that, despite the presence of the electron-donating group in proximity to the squaramide unit, exhibited strong and cooperative binding of ion pairs. The obtained sensor showed the strongest fluorescent response towards dihydrogen phosphate anions. The presence of the pyrene unit facilitated the docking of sensor 4 onto the surface of graphene quantum dots, leading to an increase in fluorescent response toward the anions tested as well as hampering deprotonation of the squaramide function by basic anions.

Unless specifically indicated, all other chemicals and reagents used in this study were purchased from commercial sources and were used as received. Solvents: acetonitrile (Merck, Steinheim, Germany, 99.9 %), chloroform (Avantor, Gliwice, Poland, 98.5 %), dichloromethane (Avantor, 99 %) methanol (Avantor, 99.8 %), tetrahydrofuran (Avantor, 99.5 %), acetonitrile‑d3 (Alfa Aesar, 99.8 %), chloroform-d (Alfa Aesar, 99.8 %), dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (Alfa Aesar, 99.5 %). Reagents: 1-aza-18-crown ether (Bujno Chemicals, 98 %), 3,4-Dimethoxy-3-cyclobutene-1,2-dione (BLDpharm, 98 %), 1-aminopyrene (Angene Chemical, 98 %), 3,5-Bis(trifluoromethyl)aniline (Merck, 97 %), 3-Nitrobenzoyl chloride (Merck, 98 %), palladium on activated charcoal 10 % Pd (Merck), triethylamine (Merck, 99 %), aniline (Merck, 99.5 %), morpholine (Merck, 99 %), N,N-Diisopropylethylamine (Merck, 99.5 %), Graphene quantum dots (Merck, blue luminescent). If necessary, product purification was performed using column chromatography on silica gel (Merck Kieselgel 60,230–400 mesh) with mixtures of chloroform/methanol. Thin-layer chromatography (TLC) was performed on silica gel plates (Merck Kieselgel 60 F254).1H and 13C spectra were recorded on a Bruker 300 MHz spectrometer. 1H NMR chemical shifts δ are reported in parts per million referenced to residual solvent signal (deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d6 ), CD3CN or CDCl3).

UV–vis titrations were performed in acetonitrile using a Thermo Spectronic Unicam UV500 Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7100 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer in CD3CN solution at 298 K. High-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were measured on a Quattro LC Micromass unit using electrospray ionization (ESI) technique.

The X-ray measurement was performed on a Bruker D8 Venture diffractometer with help of APEX3, SAINT and SADABS software. The structure was solved and refined in the SHELXTL package. The figures were prepared in Mercury software [62]. All the details concerning the measurement and the structural data are located in ESI. CCDC 2268203 contains the supplementary crystallographic data for this paper. These data can be obtained free of charge from The Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre via www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk/structures.

Compound S1. To a solution of 3,4-dimethoxy-3-cyclobutane-1,2-dione (2.00 g, 14.1 mmol) in methanol (20 ml), 3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl)aniline (2.40 ml, 14.5 mmol, 1.1 equiv.) was added at room temperature. After stirring for 3 days, the reaction mixture was filtered, and the collected solid material was washed with MeOH. The obtained light yellow solid was dried under vacuum to yield the desired product (4.54 g, 13.4 mmol, 95 %).
HRMS (ESI): calcd for C13H7F6NO3Na [M + Na]+: 362.0228, found: 362.0225.

Receptor 1. To a solution of compound S1 (204 mg, 0.60 mmol) in MeOH (5 ml), aniline (56 mg, 0.60 mmol) was added at room temperature. The mixture was stirred for 2 days. Then, the reaction mixture was filtered, and the collected solid material was washed with MeOH. The obtained white solid was dried under vacuum to yield the desired receptor (170 mg, 0.42 mmol, 71 %).
HRMS (ESI): calcd for C18H10F6N2O2Na [M + Na]+: 423.0544, found: 423.0535.

Compound S2. Morpholine (234 mg, 2.68 mmol) was added to a solution of 3-nitrobenzoyl chloride (497 mg, 2.68 mmol) and TEA (410 μl, 2.94 mmol) in dry DCM (20 ml) at 0⁰C (in an ice bath). The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min and then left at room temperature overnight. The organic phase was diluted with 10 ml DCM and washed with distilled water (2 x 20 ml), 0.5 M HCl (20 ml), and a saturated aqueous solution of NaHCO3 (30 ml), and dried over MgSO4. After evaporation of DCM, the title compound was obtained as a yellow solid (351 mg, 1.49 mmol, 55 %).
HRMS (ESI): calcd for C11H12N2O4Na [M + Na]+: 259.0694, found: 259.0681.

Receptor 2. To a degassed solution of compound S2 (350 mg, 1.48 mmol) in 45 ml of a THF/MeOH mixture (1:4), 15 mg of 10 % Pd/C was added. The reaction was kept under H2 (balloon pressure) at room temperature overnight. The catalyst was removed by filtration through a pad of Celite and washed several times with MeOH. The filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure to give the crude product in quantitative yield (305 mg). This amine was used in the next step without further purification.

To the solution of the amine (305 mg, 1.48 mmol) in 20 ml of MeOH, compound S1 (502 mg, 1.48 mmol), and triethylamine (230 μl, 1.63 mmol, 1.1 eq.) were added at room temperature. The reaction was stirred for 2 days. The mixture was filtered, and the collected solid material was washed several times with MeOH. The obtained yellow solid was dried under vacuum to yield the desired receptor 2 in a 63 % yield (480 mg, 0.93 mmol)HRMS (ESI): calcd for C23H17F6N3O4K [M + K]+: 552.0755, found: 552.0767.

Compound S3. 1-Aza-18-crown-6 (700 mg, 2.66 mmol) was added to a solution of 3-nitrobenzoyl chloride (493 mg, 2.66 mmol) and TEA (408 μl, 2.93 mmol, 1.1 eq.) in dry DCM (25 ml) at 0⁰C (ice bath). The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min and then left at room temperature overnight. The organic phase was diluted with 10 ml DCM and washed with distilled water (2 x 20 ml), 0.5 M HCl (20 ml), and a saturated aqueous solution of NaHCO3 (30 ml), and dried with MgSO4. After evaporation of DCM, the title compound was obtained as a yellow oil (746 mg, 1.80 mmol, 68 % yield).
HRMS (ESI): calcd for C19H28N2O8Na [M + Na]+: 435.1743, found: 435.1750.

Receptor 3. To a degassed solution of compound S3 (600 mg, 1.45 mmol) in 45 ml of a THF/MeOH mixture (1:4), 15 mg of 10 % Pd/C was added. The reaction was kept under H2 (balloon pressure) at room temperature overnight. The catalyst was removed by filtration through a pad of Celite and washed several times with MeOH. The filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure to give the crude product in quantitative yield (554 mg). This amine was used in the next step without further purification.

To the solution of the amine (554 mg, 1.45 mmol) in 30 ml of MeOH, compound S1 (492 mg, 1.45 mmol), and triethylamine (222 μl, 1.63 mmol, 1.1 eq.) were added at room temperature. The reaction was stirred for 1 day. The reaction mixture was concentrated under reduced pressure, giving a solid residue, which was purified by silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of MeOH in CHCl3 (5:95 v/v) to yield the target receptor 3 as a yellow solid in 60 % yield (600 mg, 0.87 mmol)HRMS (ESI): calcd for C31H33F6N3O8Na [M + Na]+: 712.2064, found: 712.2084.

Compound S4. To a degassed solution of compound S3 (500 mg, 1.21 mmol) in 45 ml of a THF/MeOH mixture (1:4), 15 mg of 10 % Pd/C was added. The reaction was kept under H2 (balloon pressure) at room temperature overnight. The catalyst was removed by filtration through a pad of Celite and washed several times with MeOH. The filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure to give the crude product in quantitative yield (463 mg). This amine was used in the next step without further purification.

To the solution of the amine (463 mg, 1.21 mmol) in 20 ml of MeOH, 3,4-dimethoxy-3-cyclobutene-1,2-dione (172 mg, 1.21 mmol), and triethylamine (185 μl, 1.33 mmol, 1.1 eq.) were added at room temperature. The reaction was stirred for 1 day. The reaction mixture was concentrated under reduced pressure, giving a solid residue, which was purified by silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of MeOH in CHCl3 (5:95 v/v) to yield the target receptor S4 as a white solid in 80 % yield (478 mg, 0.97 mmol)HRMS (ESI): calcd for C24H32N2O9Na [M + Na]+: 515.2005, found: 515.2021.

Receptor 4. To the solution of compound S4 (406 mg, 0.82 mmol) in methanol was added 1-aminopyrene (197 mg, 0.91 mmol) and N,N-diisopropylethylamine (174 μl, 0.99 mmol, 1.1 eq.) at room temperature. The reaction was stirred for 3 days. After that, the reaction mixture was concentrated, and the residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography (10 % methanol in chloroform). This process yielded receptor 4 as a yellow solid material (380 mg, 68 % yield).
HRMS (ESI): calcd for C39H39N3O8Na [M + Na]+: 700.2629, found: 700.2646.

Damian Jagleniec: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Łukasz Dobrzycki: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jan Romański: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Grant no. 2018/30/E/ST5/00841. From the National Science Centre, Poland. The X-ray structure was determined at the Advanced Crystal Engineering Laboratory (aceLAB) of the Faculty of Chemistry of the University of Warsaw.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2024.124410.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Squaramides: physical properties, synthesis and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.I. Storer, C. Aciro, L.H. Jones. Squaramides: physical properties, synthesis and applications. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40, 2330-2346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Be squared: expanding the horizon of squaric acid-mediated conjugations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.R. Wurm, H.-A. Klok, Be squared: expanding the horizon of squaric acid-mediated conjugations. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2013, 42, 822-8236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, anion recognition and transmembrane anion-transport properties of squaramides and their derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-J. Cai, Z. Li, W.-H. Chen, Synthesis, anion recognition and transmembrane anion-transport properties of squaramides and their derivatives. Mini-reviews in Organic Chemistry, 2018, 15, 148-156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The versatility of squaramides: from supramolecular chemistry to chemical biology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Marchetti, L.K. Kumawat, N. Mao, J.C. Stephens, R.B.P. Elmes, The versatility of squaramides: from supramolecular chemistry to chemical biology. Chem. 2019, 5, 1-88.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino acid-based suqaramides for anion recognition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.B.P. Elmes, K.A. Jolliffe, Amino acid-based suqaramides for anion recognition, Supramol. Chem. 2015, 27, 321-328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-affinity anion binding by steroidal squaramide receptors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino acid-based squaramides for anion recognition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.B.P. Elmes, K.A. Jolliffe, Amino acid-based squaramides for anion recognition, Supramol. Chem. 2015, 27, 321-328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Macrocyclic squaramides: anion receptors with high sulfate binding affinity and selectivity in aqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qin, A. Hartley, P. Turner, R.B.P. Elmes, K.A. Jolliffe, Macrocyclic squaramides: anion receptors with high sulfate binding affinity and selectivity in aqueous media, Chem. Sci. 2016, 7, 4563.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qin, J.R. Wright, J.D.E. Lane, S.N. Burry, R.B.P. Elmes, K.A. Jolliffe, Receptors for sulfate that function across a wide pH range in mixed aqueous-DMSO media, Chem. Commun. 2019, 55, 12312.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Picci, M. Kubicki, A. Garau, V. Lippolis, R. Mocci, A. Porcheddu, R. Quesada, P.C. Ricci, M.A. Scorciapino, C. Caltagirone, Simple squaramide receptors for highly efficient anion binding in aqueous media and transmembrane transport, Chem. Commun. 2020, 56, 11066-11069.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-J. Cai, Z. Li, W.-H. Chen, Tripodal squaramide conjugates as highly effective transmembrane anion transporters, Bioorganic and Medicinal Chemistry Letters, 2017, 27, 1999-2002.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Busschaert, S.-H. Park, K.-H. Baek, Y.P. Choi, J. Park, E.N.W. Howe, J.R. Hiscock, L.E. Karagiannidis, I. Marques, V. Félix, W. Namkung, J.L. Sessler, P.A. Gale, I. Shin, A synthetic ion transporter that disrupts autophagy and induces apoptosis by perturbating cellular chloride concentration, Nat. Chem. 2017, 9, 667-675.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kerckhoffs, M.J. Langton, Reversible photo-control over transmembrane anion transport using visible-light responsive supramolecular carriers, Chem. Sci. 2020, 11, 6325-6331.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. F. Macrae, I. Soyago, S. J. Cottrell, P. T. A. Galek, P. McCabe, E. Pidcock, M. Platings, G. P. Shields, J. S. Stevens, M. Towler and P. A. Wood, Mercury 4.0: from visualization to analysis, design and prediction, J. Appl. Ctyst., 2020, 53, 226 – 235.
